narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've built a solid foundation with your high family income background, a respectable LSAT score of 38—which is above the typical student's 36.7—and plans to attend part-time, a choice only about 7% of students make. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 falls below the average of 3.21, and your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sit at the bottom (1 out of 10), compared to typical mid-range positions around 5 or 6 for most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 41.1% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking at the bottom, signaling to the model weaker late-stage academic performance relative to peers, which strongly outweighed other strengths. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which pulled in the opposite direction by suggesting solid reasoning skills that typically help students pass. Your Year 1 decile at the bottom also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Compared to typical students who pass, your low deciles stand out as bigger hurdles than your above-average LSAT could fully offset, even with your part-time status and family support. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male with clear drive—many in similar positions succeed elsewhere—but the model weighs these patterns heavily from historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening recent academics through post-bac courses or a master's program to lift those decile concerns. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps to schools with more flexible part-time options, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. You've got advantages like your LSAT and background; targeting them strategically could flip the odds next time. Keep pushing—law needs determined voices like yours.
